title: There Is No Try
summary: Williams describes an unusually difficult environment in which a new Middle East conflict has driven headline PCE inflation up to 3-1/2 percent (with tariffs and energy adding about a point) while the labor market sends mixed signals—stable hard data at 4.3 percent unemployment but softening soft data suggesting rising slack. He judges that risks to both sides of the mandate have increased and that the FOMC's decision to hold the funds rate at 3-1/2 to 3-3/4 percent is well positioned. He expects inflation around 3 percent this year before falling to 2 percent in 2027, and stresses anchored expectations and the absence of second-round effects. With elevated inflation and supply-shock risk, the speech leans modestly hawkish-of-neutral and clearly signals a hold while the conflict's effects play out.

key_quotes.quote: The elevated levels of inflation, mixed signals from the labor market, and heightened uncertainty from the Middle East conflict present an unusual set of circumstances, but the current stance of monetary policy is well positioned to balance the risks to our maximum employment and price stability goals.
key_quotes.context: Justifying the FOMC's decision to hold rates at 3-1/2 to 3-3/4 percent

key_quotes.quote: I expect inflation to remain elevated and above the FOMC’s longer-run 2 percent goal for the next few quarters, largely due to those two factors.
key_quotes.context: On tariffs and energy prices keeping inflation elevated near term

key_quotes.quote: Right now, the future is difficult to see, and the risks to both sides of our mandate have increased.
key_quotes.context: Characterizing two-sided risk amid the Middle East conflict

key_quotes.quote: Together, these indicators suggest increasing labor market slack.
key_quotes.context: Reading the softening soft data on the labor market
